text:
Ochre. It doesn't get nearly enough attention.

Most people walk past ochre without naming it. They call it yellow-brown, or dirty gold, or just "that earthy color." But ochre has a specificity to it — a warmth that feels ancient and grounded rather than shiny or synthetic.

I associate ochre with cave paintings in Lascaux and Altamira. Humans ground this pigment from iron-rich earth tens of thousands of years ago and used it to draw animals on stone walls. That's a remarkable fact. The first color humans reached for when they wanted to record something meaningful was ochre. It predates every other artistic choice we know of.

I also associate it with late afternoon light in dry climates — the American Southwest, parts of North Africa, the Australian outback. That specific hour when sunlight goes low and warm and everything it touches looks like it was painted with the same pigment those early humans were grinding.

Ochre sits between yellow and brown in a way that feels honest. It doesn't try to be vivid or impressive. It just holds its ground quietly, like old stone, like dried grass, like soil that has been there a very long time.

It deserves more credit.
